%% By default, the full list of authors will be used in the page

\renewcommand{\shortauthors}{Roth et al.}

%%
%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
\begin{abstract}

Emulating Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) campaigns in controlled
environments requires behavioral knowledge of the adversary and the
correct configuration of the \emph{System Under Test} (SUT), the
target environment where procedures will run.
Despite the broad adoption of the \mitre{} \attack{} framework,
encoded in \stix{} bundles, the information necessary to derive
concrete SUT requirements (software versions, dependencies,
exploitable vulnerabilities, and infrastructure topology) is
rarely expressed in structured form.
In this work, we perform a systematic measurement analysis of the
\attack{} Enterprise, Mobile, and ICS datasets to quantify the
availability, completeness, and specificity of SUT-relevant
information. For cross-corpus coverage, we also measure CAPEC and
FiGHT using the same active-object filtering rules.
We define a SUT-centric requirements model and coverage, sparsity,
and deployment compatibility metrics that classify technique,
software, and vulnerability constraints according to their
feasibility in containers, virtual machines, or full
infrastructure.
Our results show that platform constraints are broadly present
but coarse, software references rarely include specific
versions, and vulnerability information is sparse and
fragmented across structured and free-text fields. This makes automated SUT
generation infeasible from structured CTI alone. These results
define the boundary between descriptive behavioral intelligence
and the environment specifications needed for reproducible
emulation.

\end{abstract}

% ===============================================================
\section{Introduction}\label{sec:introduction}

Controlled APT emulation requires two things: a description of
\emph{what} an adversary does, and a concrete environment in which
to do it. The first is increasingly well served by the \mitre{} \attack{}
knowledge base, which now catalogues 835 Enterprise techniques across 187
documented groups and 52 campaigns, all encoded in
machine-readable \stix{} bundles~\cite{strom2018mitre,apurva2024mitre}.
The second remains an open problem. Every emulation run requires a
\emph{System Under Test (SUT)}: a configured environment whose OS
family, installed software, exposed services, trust relationships,
and vulnerable package versions must be specified before a single
technique can execute. SUT choice determines which procedures are
reachable, which privilege escalation paths exist, and which CVEs
are exploitable. Without a correct SUT, even a perfectly faithful
procedure description produces either a silent no-op or an
unreproducible result.

The central question this paper asks is simple:
\emph{Can structured CTI provide enough information to derive
SUT requirements automatically, and if not, where exactly does it
fall short?} We answer this question by measuring
every SUT-relevant field across the \attack{} Enterprise, Mobile,
and ICS corpora, and extend the coverage comparison to CAPEC and
FiGHT\@. Our findings are clear:
platform constraints are broadly present but coarse; software
references rarely carry version specificity or CPE identifiers;
and CVE linkages are sparse and split across structured and
free-text encoding.
Automated SUT generation from structured CTI alone is not feasible
under current encoding practices.

The gap has practical consequences for the evaluation stack.
Red team platforms such as \caldera{} provide execution logic but
leave SUT instantiation entirely to the
analyst~\cite{applebaum2016caldera,redcanary2023art,orbinato2024laccolith,damodaran2025automated,wang2024sands}. APT detection systems are
meaningfully evaluated only for techniques that can actually reach a
target~\cite{milajerdi2019holmes,han2020unicorn,DBLP:conf/uss/Dong0NS0LLX23,DBLP:conf/uss/YangXXLZ23,Li_2024,DBLP:conf/ccs/LvGQCZCJ24,DBLP:conf/sp/RehmanAH24,Cheng_2024,DBLP:conf/uss/JiaXN0ZW24,DBLP:conf/ccs/Aly0Y25,DBLP:conf/uss/Zhang0YK0KF25}. Attribution research shows
that robust actor-level conclusions remain difficult under TTP overlap,
heterogeneous evidence quality, and incomplete reporting~\cite{DBLP:conf/uss/SahaMBCVL25,DBLP:conf/ccs/YuldoshkhujaevJ25}.
Together, these observations increase dependence on richer contextual
evidence (especially software and vulnerability context), precisely
the signals most often missing from structured encoding. The same
operational tension appears in recent analyses of ATT\&CK-oriented
evaluation practice and operational deployment constraints in
industry~\cite{apurva2024mitre,10.1145/3634737.3645012,DBLP:conf/ccs/DongLJLWHXCLGC23}.

A CTI standard that names malware but not its version, or
references a CVE only in a prose description, cannot drive
automated SUT construction. That is the problem this paper
measures.

\myparagraph{Where structured CTI falls short}
CTI encodes \emph{what} was observed: which techniques appeared
in a campaign, which malware was used, which groups were involved.
It does not encode \emph{where} it ran. Translating a \stix{}
bundle into a deployable environment requires knowing which OS
version hosts a service, which package version carries a
vulnerability, and which network topology enables lateral movement.
None of these are structured fields in current \attack{} encoding.

The \attack{} framework is the natural target for this measurement: it is widely
adopted as a structured CTI standard and used as input by red
team platforms, detection evaluations, and threat hunting
programs~\cite{strom2018mitre,apurva2024mitre,DBLP:conf/uss/VirkudIR00024}. Its design as a behavioral
taxonomy rather than an environment
specification~\cite{strom2018mitre,ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured}
is correct and intentional, but it creates a gap when
practitioners treat it as sufficient input for emulation
workflows that require both. We measure that gap.

Our companion study measured the procedural axis, that is, whether
structured CTI encodes enough detail to execute attack steps in order.
This paper isolates the environment axis: whether structured CTI encodes
enough detail to build the target environment in which those steps can run.
Separating these axes avoids conflating missing procedure details with
missing SUT requirements.

\myparagraph{Research questions}

\noindent\hangindent=2.7em\hangafter=1
\textbf{RQ1 (Coverage):} How much SUT-relevant information is
present in structured CTI objects and relationships, and how
complete is it for deriving environment prerequisites such as
platforms, software dependencies, and vulnerabilities?

\noindent\hangindent=2.7em\hangafter=1
\textbf{RQ2 (Deployability):} To what extent can software and
vulnerability references be transformed into deployable SUT
specifications for containers, VMs, or IaC, and what classes
of requirements remain unmappable?

\noindent\hangindent=2.7em\hangafter=1
\textbf{RQ3 (Specificity):} How specific are SUT requirement
profiles with respect to known intrusion sets and campaigns, and
how reliably can they support susceptibility assessment without
over-claiming attribution?

\myparagraph{Contributions}

\stepone~We define a SUT-centric measurement model that
operationalizes CTI into environment prerequisites, covering
platform, software, and vulnerability constraints.

\steptwo~A corpus-scale measurement quantifies the availability, sparsity, and ambiguity of
SUT-relevant signals across \stix{} objects and relationships in
the \mitre{} \attack{} Enterprise, Mobile, and ICS datasets,
with cross-corpus coverage also measured for CAPEC and FiGHT\@.

\stepthree~A compatibility taxonomy classifies
techniques by their minimum virtualization backend requirement
(container-feasible, VM-required, or infrastructure-dependent).

\stepfour~We analyze SUT profile specificity across intrusion sets
and discuss the limitations imposed by under-reporting and shared
commodity behaviors~\cite{DBLP:conf/uss/SahaMBCVL25}.

\myparagraph{Organization}
The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.
Section~\ref{sec:background} presents ATT\&CK and STIX background;
Section~\ref{sec:related} reviews related work;
Section~\ref{sec:definitions} defines the SUT measurement model;
Section~\ref{sec:dataset} describes the datasets;
Section~\ref{sec:methodology} details the measurement pipeline;
Section~\ref{sec:analysis} reports the results;
Section~\ref{sec:discussion} discusses implications and threats to validity;
and Section~\ref{sec:conclusion} concludes.

% ===============================================================
\section{Background}\label{sec:background}

\subsection{The MITRE ATT\&CK Framework}

The \mitre{} \attack{} framework is a curated knowledge base
documenting adversary tactics and techniques observed in real-world
cyber operations. It organizes adversary behavior into a matrix of
tactics (attacker objectives) and techniques (concrete methods),
with sub-techniques capturing more specific procedural variants.
The framework is divided into three domains: Enterprise (Windows,
Linux, macOS, cloud, network infrastructure), Mobile, and
Industrial Control Systems (ICS). The Enterprise domain is the
most extensively documented, containing 835 techniques and
sub-techniques across 14 tactics as of v18.1~\cite{strom2018mitre,apurva2024mitre}.

\subsection{CTI Representation in STIX}

ATT\&CK content is distributed as \stix{} 2.x
bundles~\cite{stixspec}.
STIX defines a graph-based
interchange format in which entities are typed STIX Domain Objects
(SDOs) and connections are STIX Relationship Objects (SROs). The
objects most relevant to SUT construction are
\texttt{attack-pattern} (techniques), \texttt{campaign},
\texttt{intrusion-set}, \texttt{malware}, \texttt{tool}, and
\texttt{relationship}. SROs encode semantic associations such as
\emph{uses}, \emph{attributed-to}, and \emph{targets}, without
specifying execution order, preconditions, or environment bindings.

The \stix{} schema does not define a structured procedure object.
Ordering, environmental requirements, and parameter flows appear
only in natural-language \texttt{description} fields and are not
machine-readable. This design reflects the role of \attack{} as a
behavioral taxonomy rather than a machine-actionable execution
specification~\cite{strom2018mitre,ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured}.

\subsection{Key Entities and SUT Relevance}

Table~\ref{tab:entities} summarizes the \attack{} entity types
most relevant to SUT construction.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\caption{ATT\&CK entity types and their SUT relevance.}\label{tab:entities}
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{2.0cm} >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{5.6cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Entity} & \textbf{SUT relevance} \\
\midrule
\texttt{attack-pattern} &
  Platform hints via \texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms};
  system requirements in \texttt{x\_mitre\_system\_requirements}
  (rarely populated). \\
\texttt{malware} /
\texttt{tool} &
  Implies OS dependency; version rarely specified;
  CPE mappings largely absent. \\
\texttt{campaign} &
  Scopes operation to time window and target set;
  links to techniques and intrusion set. \\
\texttt{intrusion-set} &
  Aggregates TTP profile; useful for SUT susceptibility
  profiling but not for single-campaign instantiation. \\
\texttt{vulnerability} &
  CVE-mapped objects present in some bundles;
  rarely linked directly to campaigns. \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\subsection{ATT\&CK in Adversary Emulation}

Red team platforms such as \caldera{} provide an execution
substrate for adversary emulation but assume a compatible SUT
already exists. They do not derive environment configurations from
CTI\@. Every ability requires a concrete command, and execution
failures expose missing assumptions in the CTI-derived
representation rather than framework
deficiencies~\cite{applebaum2016caldera}.

SUT construction therefore remains a manual step that precedes
emulation, and the quality of that SUT determines whether emulated
procedures can succeed.
This background motivates the core question of this paper:
which parts of SUT construction are already supported by structured CTI,
and which parts still require manual inference.

% ===============================================================
\section{Related Work}\label{sec:related}
% Note: poster papers are tracked for ideation only and are intentionally
% excluded from citation claims in this manuscript.
% Citation governance note (quality scope):
% - Core evidence is top-4, arXiv, or standards.
% - The following non-top4 citations are intentionally retained because they
%   are either companion/internal baseline, foundational tooling, or
%   high-quality adjacent venues directly supporting specific claims:
%   10.1145/3634737.3645012, cheng2025ctinexus, shen2025pentestagent,
%   savat2021extractor, vermeer2022evolution, applebaum2016caldera,
%   orbinato2024laccolith, strom2018mitre, redcanary2023art,
%   ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured.

\myparagraph{CTI completeness and measurement}
Jin et al.\ show that CTI sharing provides limited operational
value partly because shared feeds exhibit inconsistent field usage
and low structural completeness~\cite{DBLP:conf/ndss/JinKLBKK24}.
Vermeer et al.\ measure gaps in STIX-encoded
sources~\cite{vermeer2022evolution}. Prior work surveys ATT\&CK's
design, adoption, and known
limitations~\cite{strom2018mitre,apurva2024mitre}; they identify sparse
software versioning as an open problem but do not quantify it.
Recent provenance-graph APT detection systems also show that
operational coverage depends on what telemetry and ATT\&CK-linked
behaviors are representable in the underlying datasets, and not just on
model architecture~\cite{milajerdi2019holmes,han2020unicorn,DBLP:conf/uss/Dong0NS0LLX23,DBLP:conf/uss/YangXXLZ23,Li_2024,DBLP:conf/uss/JiaXN0ZW24,DBLP:conf/uss/Zhang0YK0KF25}. All of these works measure
\emph{behavioral} completeness. None asks whether the encoded
data is sufficient to instantiate the environment where emulation
must run.

\myparagraph{CTI extraction pipelines and machine-actionability}
Recent CTI extraction pipelines improve structured recovery of
behavioral intelligence from reports, including TTP extraction and
graph construction~\cite{savat2021extractor,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2412-10872,cheng2025ctinexus,DBLP:conf/uss/BuchelPLCZBE0GC25}.
However, these lines of work still prioritize \emph{what} happened
over \emph{where} execution should occur. As a result, they do not
provide a measurement framework for SUT derivation quality.

\myparagraph{Behavioral profiling and attribution specificity}
Practitioner-centered attribution research reports that analysts often
prioritize understanding adversary TTPs, intent, and operational context
over confidently naming a specific actor, due to ambiguity in available
evidence and collaboration frictions~\cite{DBLP:conf/uss/SahaMBCVL25}. Longitudinal
analysis of public APT dossiers likewise highlights heterogeneity in report
quality and evidence granularity across sources and periods~\cite{DBLP:conf/ccs/YuldoshkhujaevJ25}.
Complementary evidence from ATT\&CK-oriented behavior specificity analysis
reinforces this ambiguity at the group-discrimination
level~\cite{saha2026kittenpandameasuringspecificity}.
These findings motivate our focus on \emph{deployability}: whether
software and CVE references carry enough structured information to
instantiate a runnable target environment, independent of hard
attribution claims.

\myparagraph{Adversary emulation frameworks}
Tools such as \caldera{}~\cite{applebaum2016caldera}, Atomic Red
Team~\cite{redcanary2023art}, and planning-based systems such as
AURORA~\cite{wang2024sands} assume a compatible SUT and provide
execution logic. None derives environment specifications from
CTI automatically. Laccolith~\cite{orbinato2024laccolith} and
LLM-driven proposals~\cite{singer2025incalmo,deng2024pentestgpt,shen2025pentestagent} specify SUTs manually
or rely on fixed lab topologies. Our work characterizes what CTI
can contribute to this automation gap.

\myparagraph{Cyber range and testbed design}
Cyber range frameworks rely on reproducible environment
definitions that specify OS images, service configurations, network
topology, and vulnerable software. These specifications are authored
manually and not derived from
CTI~\cite{Chen_2020,orbinato2024laccolith,damodaran2025automated}. Our findings quantify which CTI
fields could, in principle, feed such specifications and which
cannot, thereby bounding the scope of automated SUT derivation.
Poster-scale studies in adjacent venues also explore ATT\&CK-grounded
automation for specialized environments (e.g., ICS malware analysis),
but without structured SUT derivation from CTI
bundles~\cite{DBLP:conf/asiaccs/KurtSKT25}. We use these results as
contextual signals, not as primary evidence.

\myparagraph{The gap this paper fills}
The studies above collectively characterize CTI quality from a
\emph{behavioral} perspective, including coverage of techniques,
attribution specificity, TTP extraction. To the best of our knowledge,
prior studies do not explicitly measure whether
structured CTI contains the environmental information required to
instantiate the target system where emulation runs. The
compatibility class of a technique (container, VM, or full
infrastructure), the version specificity of referenced software,
and the deployability of CVE constraints are not systematically measured
in this problem setting. This paper provides those measurements for the
first time, across five ATT\&CK-derived corpora, establishing a
quantitative foundation for the design of CTI standards that
support automated SUT generation.
Section~\ref{sec:definitions} turns this gap into an explicit
measurement model and operational metrics.

% ===============================================================
\section{SUT Measurement Model}\label{sec:definitions}

We use a SUT-centric measurement model that operationalizes structured
CTI objects into environment prerequisites. The model is intentionally
minimal: it defines which constraints are required to instantiate an
environment, and how those constraints are measured across datasets.

\subsection{SUT Constraint Types}

\myparagraph{Platform constraints}
A technique carries a platform constraint when
\texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms} lists a supported OS family, cloud
provider, or device type. This is the coarsest SUT signal: it
indicates compatibility class, but not version, configuration, or
privilege model.

\myparagraph{Software constraints}
A software constraint is induced when a \texttt{malware}
or \texttt{tool} object is linked to a technique or campaign via a
\emph{uses} relationship type. Software constraints imply the presence
of an installable artifact in the SUT\@. Their specificity depends
on whether the object carries a version identifier or CPE
reference; absent these fields, the constraint is satisfiable only
at the family level.

\myparagraph{Vulnerability constraints}
A vulnerability constraint is induced when a CVE
identifier appears in object descriptions or when a
\texttt{vulnerability} object is linked to a campaign, technique,
or software artifact. Vulnerability constraints are the most
specific: they identify a product, affected version range, and
attack surface. They are also the most actionable for SUT
construction: deploying the vulnerable product at the specified
version directly satisfies the constraint.

\subsection{SUT Requirement Profile}

For each campaign (and analogously for each intrusion set), we define a
SUT requirement profile as the union of three components:
(i) platform constraints induced by associated techniques,
(ii) software constraints induced by linked malware/tool objects, and
(iii) vulnerability constraints induced by CVE evidence.
This profile is used only as an analysis artifact for coverage and
specificity comparisons; it is not presented as an execution plan.

\subsection{SUT Compatibility Classes}

We classify each technique by the minimum virtualization backend
required to satisfy its constraints:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Container-feasible (CF):} user-space artifacts
    with no kernel-module, firmware, or Active Directory dependency.
  \item \textbf{VM-required (VMR):} techniques involving kernel
    interactions, process injection, or boot-level persistence.
  \item \textbf{Infrastructure-dependent (ID):} behaviors
    presupposing multi-host topology, domain controllers, or
    enterprise identity services.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Measurement Metrics}

\myparagraph{Platform coverage}
Fraction of techniques with at least one entry in
\texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms}.

\myparagraph{Software reference rate}
Fraction of campaigns and intrusion sets linked to at least one
\texttt{malware} or \texttt{tool} object.

\myparagraph{Vulnerability reference rate}
Fraction of campaigns and intrusion sets with at least one CVE
linkage (direct or via associated software).

\myparagraph{SUT profile specificity}
Fraction of intrusion sets whose SUT profiles are unique under Jaccard
distance threshold $\delta = 0.1$ when compared to all other
intrusion set profiles.

% ===============================================================
\section{Dataset}\label{sec:dataset}

\subsection{Primary Dataset}

Following the measurement model in Section~\ref{sec:definitions},
we select datasets that expose the same core STIX structures while
covering different ATT\&CK scopes.
We use the \mitre{} \attack{} Enterprise bundle v18.1 as the
primary source for all quantitative analyses. This bundle is the
most extensively documented domain and is evaluated jointly with
the companion study on procedural
semantics~\cite{ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured},
which uses the same bundle version.

Table~\ref{tab:dataset} summarizes object counts across all analyzed
sources.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\caption{STIX object counts across ATT\&CK domains and sources
         (total bundle counts; active counts after deprecation
         filtering are reported in Section~\ref{sec:methodology}).}\label{tab:dataset}
\centering\footnotesize
\begin{tabular}{lrrrrr}
\toprule
\textbf{Source} &
\textbf{Techniques} &
\textbf{Camp.} &
\textbf{IS} &
\textbf{Malware} &
\textbf{Tools} \\
\midrule
Enterprise & 835 & 52  & 187 & 696 & 91 \\
Mobile     & 190 & 3   & 17  & 121 & 2  \\
ICS        & 95  & 8   & 16  & 30  & 0  \\
CAPEC      & 615 & 0   & 0   & 0   & 0 \\
FiGHT      & 707 & 0   & 136 & 475 & 73 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The quantitative pipeline in this artifact traverses all five local
bundles for cross-corpus coverage metrics. RQ2 and RQ3 remain focused
on Enterprise because only Enterprise contains campaign coverage
sufficient for the deployment-focused analyses.
CAPEC is included as STIX-encoded attack-pattern knowledge for
coverage contrast only; in this setup it is not a campaign-level CTI
corpus.

The Enterprise bundle contains 20,048 relationship objects.
After excluding 144 deprecated or revoked \texttt{attack-pattern}
objects, 15 deprecated \texttt{intrusion-set} objects, and
3 deprecated \texttt{malware} objects, our analysis covers
691 active techniques, 52 campaigns, 172 intrusion sets, and
784 software artifacts (693 malware~$+$~91 tools).
The same bundle version and object-filtering conventions are used
in the companion
study~\cite{ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured}.

\subsection{Extracted Fields}

For each \texttt{attack-pattern} object, we extract
\texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms},
\texttt{x\_mitre\_system\_requirements},
\texttt{x\_mitre\_permissions\_required}, and
\texttt{x\_mitre\_defense\_bypassed}. For \texttt{malware} and
\texttt{tool} objects, we extract \texttt{name}, \texttt{aliases},
\texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms}, and any CPE or version strings in
\texttt{external\_references}. CVE identifiers are extracted from
\texttt{description} fields and from linked
\texttt{vulnerability} objects.

% ===============================================================
\section{Measurement Methodology}\label{sec:methodology}

\subsection{Extraction Pipeline}

We parse the locally available \stix{} bundles (Enterprise, Mobile,
and ICS) as JSON and
normalize object identifiers. Objects marked as deprecated
(\texttt{x\_mitre\_deprecated}) or revoked (\texttt{revoked}) are
excluded; this removes 144 of the 835 \texttt{attack-pattern}
objects in the Enterprise bundle, leaving 691 active techniques.
To preserve comparability with the companion paper, we keep the same
bundle version, object-filtering rules, and relationship traversal
strategy used in the procedural-gap baseline~\cite{ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured}.
Relationships are indexed by
(\textit{source\_ref}, \textit{relationship\_type},
\textit{target\_ref}) triples to enable bidirectional traversal.
For each campaign, the technique set is collected from all
\texttt{attack-pattern} objects reachable via \emph{uses}
relationships; software and CVE associations are derived
analogously.
For campaign-level target platform inference, we use a conservative
rule: platforms are derived only from \texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms}
attached to software (\texttt{malware}/\texttt{tool}) linked to the
campaign. Technique-level platforms are excluded from this step
because they encode where a technique can run in general, not what a
specific campaign targeted.
Campaigns with no linked software are reported as \emph{platform
unknown} instead of receiving inferred values. This avoids
overstating environment evidence when the bundle lacks software-level
signals.

\subsection{SUT Compatibility Classification}

Each technique is assigned a compatibility class in order of
precedence:
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.2em,itemsep=0.2em,topsep=0.2em]
\item If \texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms} contains only
      \texttt{Containers} or \texttt{Linux} and the technique does not
      belong to kernel-interaction or boot-persistence clusters:
      \textbf{CF}.
\item If \texttt{x\_mitre\_permissions\_required} contains
      \texttt{Administrator}, \texttt{SYSTEM}, or \texttt{root}, or the
      technique falls in privilege-escalation or boot/firmware
      defense-evasion: \textbf{VMR}.
\item If \texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms} references
      \texttt{Windows Domain} or \texttt{Identity Provider}, or the
      technique is Lateral Movement with an Active Directory software
      dependency: \textbf{ID}.
\item Default: \textbf{VMR}.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Profile Specificity Analysis}

For each intrusion set $g$ we encode its SUT profile as a binary vector
over all software and CVE identifiers in the corpus. Pairwise
Jaccard distances are computed and the fraction of groups with at
least one neighbor within $\delta = 0.1$ is reported as the SUT
profile confusion rate.

% ===============================================================
\section{Analysis}\label{sec:analysis}
% LTeX: enabled=false

% ── Pipeline output (2026-03-05, ATT&CK Enterprise v18.1) ──────────
% Fixed denominators (active, non-deprecated/revoked objects):
% - Enterprise techniques: 691  (835 total minus 144 deprecated/revoked)
% - Campaigns: 52               (all active; C0033 has 1 uses relationship)
% - Intrusion sets: 172         (187 total minus 15 deprecated/revoked)
% - Software (malware+tools): 784  (693 malware + 91 tools)
% Always report count and percentage together.
% Quick check before submission:
% - CF + VMR + ID = 691    ✓ (19 + 526 + 146 = 691)
% - Partition percentages ≈ 100%  ✓ (2.7 + 76.1 + 21.1 = 99.9)
% Keep one decimal place for percentages, except when value < 1.0%.
% Pipeline script: ../measurement/sut/scripts/sut_measurement_pipeline.py
% Audit CSVs:     ../measurement/sut/scripts/results/audit/

Results are reported in the same order as the research questions:
coverage first (RQ1), deployability and compatibility second (RQ2),
and profile specificity last (RQ3).

\subsection{Platform Constraint Coverage (RQ1)}
% TASK (RQ1/platform):
% 1) Compute rho_P for measured corpora and report all five bundles.
% 2) Compute fill rate of x_mitre_system_requirements.
% 3) Confirm filters (deprecated/revoked) match Methods.

Of the \enterpriseplatformcount{} active Enterprise techniques, all
\enterpriseplatformcount{} (\enterpriseplatformpct\%) carry at
least one entry in \texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms}---platform annotation
is effectively mandatory in current ATT\&CK curation. However, the
\texttt{x\_mitre\_system\_requirements} field, the most
specific platform annotation, is populated in
\enterprisesystemrequirementscount{} techniques
(\enterprisesystemrequirementspct\%), confirming that fine-grained OS and
software version constraints are entirely absent from the structured
representation. Platform coverage is similarly complete in the Mobile
(\mobileplatformpct\%) and ICS (\icsplatformpercentage\%) domains.
The CAPEC bundle does not use
\texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms}, reflecting its focus on abstract attack
patterns rather than platform-bound behaviors. In FiGHT, platform
coverage is \fightplatformpercentage\% under the same active-object filter.
Figure~\ref{fig:coverage} compares measured rates across all corpora.
The CAPEC zeros in this figure are structural properties of the
bundle (no \texttt{malware}/\texttt{tool} objects and no CVE mentions in
active \texttt{attack-pattern} objects), not missing data caused by the
measurement pipeline.

\begin{figure}[!htb]
\centering
\resizebox{\columnwidth}{!}{\input{figs/coverage_template.tex}}
% FIGURE NOTE:
% - Keep Y-axis fixed at 0 to 100.
% - Add value labels.
\caption{Coverage of structured SUT-relevant signals across corpora. CAPEC zeros are schema-driven in this dataset.}\label{fig:coverage}
\Description{Grouped bar chart for Enterprise, Mobile, ICS, CAPEC, and FiGHT with measured platform, software-link, and CVE-link rates.}
\end{figure}

\myparagraph{Campaign-level platform signal under software-only inference}
Applying the software-only platform rule to all
\enterpriseactivecampaigncount{} Enterprise
campaigns yields a platform signal for
\enterprisecampaignswithplatformsignalcount{} campaigns
(\enterprisecampaignswithplatformsignalpct\%); the
remaining \enterprisecampaignsplatformunknowncount{} campaigns
(\enterprisecampaignsplatformunknownpct\%) are genuinely unknown under structured
fields alone: \emph{FrostyGoop Incident}, \emph{KV Botnet Activity},
\emph{Leviathan Australian Intrusions}, \emph{SPACEHOP Activity}, and
\emph{ShadowRay}. Table~\ref{tab:campaign_os_families} summarizes the
OS-family distribution among campaigns with signal. Campaigns can map
to multiple families.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\caption{Software-derived OS-family evidence across Enterprise campaigns (multi-label counts).}\label{tab:campaign_os_families}
\centering\scriptsize
\begin{tabular}{lr}
\toprule
\textbf{OS family} & \textbf{Campaigns} \\
\midrule
Windows & \campaignoswindowscount{} \\
Linux   & \campaignoslinuxcount{} \\
macOS   & \campaignosmacoscount{} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\subsection{Software Reference Rate (RQ1, RQ2)}
% TASK (RQ1/RQ2 software):
% 1) Measure software-link rates for campaigns and intrusion sets.
% 2) Detect version signal from name/alias/external_references.
% 3) Count software objects that include CPE.

The Enterprise bundle contains \enterpriseactivemalwarecount{} active
malware and \enterpriseactivetoolcount{} tool objects
(\enterpriseactivesoftwarecount{} total). Of the
\enterpriseactivecampaigncount{} campaigns,
\enterprisecampaignswithsoftwarecount{} (\enterprisecampaignswithsoftwarepercentage\%) are linked to at
least one software artifact (95\% confidence interval:
\enterprisecampaignswithsoftwarecilow{}\% to
\enterprisecampaignswithsoftwarecihigh{}\%). Among the
\enterpriseactiveintrusionsetcount{} active intrusion sets,
\enterpriseintrusionsetswithsoftwarecount{} (\enterpriseintrusionsetswithsoftwarepercentage\%) carry at least one software association. Of
all \enterpriseactivesoftwarecount{} software objects,
only \softwarewithversionsignalpercentage\% contain any form of version specifier in their
\texttt{name}, \texttt{aliases}, or \texttt{external\_references}
fields (95\% confidence interval: \softwarewithversionsignalcilow{}\% to
\softwarewithversionsignalcihigh{}\%). CPE identifiers, which directly enable
version-pinned installation, are present in \softwarewithcpepercentage\% of
software objects. While software \emph{presence} is broadly
documented, the specificity required for automated SUT
construction is entirely absent.
The distribution of specificity levels is summarized in
Figure~\ref{fig:software_specificity}.
The key reading is that almost all software references are family-level
labels without a version pin.

\begin{figure}[!htb]
\centering
\resizebox{\columnwidth}{!}{\input{figs/software_specificity_template.tex}}
% FIGURE NOTE:
% - Stacked bar must sum to 100%.
% - Include total N in caption or legend.
\caption{Specificity of software references linked to campaigns and intrusion sets.}\label{fig:software_specificity}
\Description{Stacked bar with three segments: software references without version and without CPE, with textual version but no CPE, and with CPE, using measured counts and percentages.}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Vulnerability Reference Rate (RQ1, RQ2)}
% TASK (RQ1/RQ2 CVE):
% 1) Extract CVEs from structured fields and free text with one parser.
% 2) Deduplicate CVE IDs before counting.
% 3) Report campaign/group coverage and structured vs text split.

CVE identifiers extracted from the Enterprise bundle total
\cveuniquecount{} unique CVEs. The bundle contains zero dedicated
\texttt{vulnerability} SDOs; all CVE evidence resides either in
\texttt{external\_references} entries or in free-text
\texttt{description} fields. Of the \cveuniquecount{}~CVEs,
\cvetechniqueonlycount{} appear only
as illustrative examples inside technique descriptions (e.g.,
\emph{Exploit Public-Facing Application} citing CVE-2016-6662);
these are not campaign-specific. The remaining
\cveactionablecount{} are
\emph{actionable}: linked to specific software, campaign, or
intrusion-set objects. Only \entcampaignswithcvecount{} campaigns
(\entcampaignswithcvepct\%, 95\% confidence interval:
\entcampaignswithcvecilow{}\% to \entcampaignswithcvecihigh{}\%)
carry any CVE reference, and only
\entintrusionsetswithcvecount{} intrusion
sets (\entintrusionsetswithcvepct\%) are associated with at least one CVE\@. These
numbers are substantially lower than the 31.6\% CVE coverage
reported for ATT\&CK groups by Saha et al., who relied on
downloading and parsing full threat reports rather than structured
STIX fields alone~\cite{DBLP:conf/uss/SahaMBCVL25}, confirming the
structural gap between referenced intelligence and encoded
intelligence.
Figure~\ref{fig:cve_location} shows where CVE evidence is found in
practice. The key reading is that actionable CVE evidence is sparse and
split between structured and free-text fields.

\myparagraph{Campaign-linked exploited CVEs}
Among the \cveactionablecount{} actionable CVEs,
\campaignlinkedcvecount{} are explicitly tied to campaign
objects in the Enterprise STIX dataset. These CVEs appear in five campaigns and
represent the strongest structured vulnerability evidence for SUT
instantiation in the current bundle.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\caption{Campaign-linked exploited CVEs in Enterprise STIX, version 18.1.}\label{tab:campaign_linked_cves}
\centering\scriptsize
\begin{tabular}{>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{4.2cm} >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{3.0cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Campaign} & \textbf{CVEs} \\
\midrule
APT28 Nearest Neighbor Campaign & CVE-2022-38028 \\
ShadowRay & CVE-2023-48022 \\
Operation MidnightEclipse & CVE-2024-3400 \\
Versa Director Zero Day Exploitation & CVE-2024-39717 \\
SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation & CVE-2025-49704; CVE-2025-49706; CVE-2025-53770; CVE-2025-53771 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{figure}[!htb]
\centering
\resizebox{\columnwidth}{!}{\input{figs/cve_location_template.tex}}
% FIGURE NOTE:
% - Show count and percentage on bars.
% - Keep parser version in script header.
\caption{Location of CVE evidence in the Enterprise corpus.}\label{fig:cve_location}
\Description{Two horizontal bars comparing measured CVE evidence found in structured fields versus free-text descriptions.}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Initial Access Signals (RQ1 Supplement)}

The Enterprise corpus contains \initialaccesstechniquecount{}
active techniques in the Initial Access tactic. At campaign level,
\campaignswithinitialaccesscount{} of
\enterpriseactivecampaigncount{} campaigns
(\campaignswithinitialaccesspct\%, 95\% confidence interval:
\campaignswithinitialaccesscilow{}\% to
\campaignswithinitialaccesscihigh{}\%) are linked to at least one
Initial Access technique.

To avoid over-interpretation, we report a conservative
\emph{social-interaction proxy}: campaigns linked to
T1566.* (Phishing family) or T1199 (Trusted Relationship).
Under this rule,
\campaignswithsocialinitialaccesscount{} campaigns
(\campaignswithsocialinitialaccesspct\%)
show social-interaction Initial Access evidence.

When intersecting Initial Access with campaign-level CVE evidence,
\campaignswithinitialaccessandcvecount{} campaigns
(\campaignswithinitialaccessandcvepct\%) have both signals, while
\campaignswithinitialaccessnocvecount{} campaigns
(\campaignswithinitialaccessnocvepct\%) have Initial Access
without any campaign-level CVE evidence. This indicates that, in
structured ATT\&CK encoding, Initial Access is often documented
without explicit exploit identifiers.

\subsection{SUT Compatibility Classification (RQ2)}
% TASK (RQ2 compatibility):
% 1) Apply CF/VMR/ID rules exactly as in Methodology.
% 2) Export class per technique for audit.
% 3) Confirm totals and percentages match Table~\ref{tab:compat}.

Table~\ref{tab:compat} reports the distribution of the
\enterpriseplatformcount{} active
Enterprise techniques across SUT compatibility classes.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\caption{SUT compatibility classification of Enterprise techniques
         (n = \enterpriseplatformcount{}).}\label{tab:compat}
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{lrr}
\toprule
\textbf{Class} & \textbf{Techniques} & \textbf{Fraction} \\
\midrule
Container-feasible (CF)        & \compatibilitycontainerfeasiblecount{} & \compatibilitycontainerfeasiblepercentage\% \\
VM-required (VMR)              & \compatibilityvmrequiredcount{} & \compatibilityvmrequiredpercentage\% \\
Infrastructure-dependent (ID)  & \compatibilityinfrastructuredependentcount{} & \compatibilityinfrastructuredependentpercentage\% \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Techniques classified as VMR or ID cannot be faithfully reproduced
in a container-only environment. Campaigns involving Lateral
Movement, boot-level persistence, or Credential Access through
domain services consistently fall into VMR or ID classes, limiting
the applicability of container-based cyber ranges for full-campaign
emulation.

\subsection{SUT Profile Specificity (RQ3)}
% TASK (RQ3 specificity):
% 1) Build profiles in two settings: software-only and software+CVE.
% 2) Compute nearest-neighbor Jaccard distance and confusion at delta=0.1.
% 3) Recompute unique-profile percentages from the same matrix.

Of the \enterpriseactiveintrusionsetcount{} active intrusion sets,
\sutprofileuniquesoftwarepercentage\% possess a unique SUT profile
under Jaccard distance $\delta = 0.1$ when profiles are built from
software constraints alone. This fraction remains at \sutprofileuniquesoftwarecvepercentage\%
when CVE constraints are added---unsurprising given that only
\entintrusionsetswithcvecount{} intrusion sets carry any CVE linkage. The remaining \sutprofileconfusionsoftwarecvepercentage\% of groups still
share their SUT profile with at least one other group. A SUT built
to satisfy the profile of group $g$ therefore also satisfies the
profiles of all groups within distance $\delta$, making it
impossible to claim that a successful emulation is specific to the
targeted adversary. This SUT profile confusion parallels the
attribution ambiguity documented at the behavioral level by Saha
et al.~\cite{DBLP:conf/uss/SahaMBCVL25}, but operates at the
\emph{environmental} level. A bootstrap stability check with 5{,}000
replicates at $\delta = 0.10$ yields a confusion estimate of
\bootstrapconfusionpct\% (confidence interval:
\bootstrapconfusioncilow{}\%--\bootstrapconfusioncihigh{}\%) and
unique-profile estimate of \bootstrapuniquepct\% (confidence interval:
\bootstrapuniquecilow{}\%--\bootstrapuniquecihigh{}\%).
Table~\ref{tab:ablation_rq3} summarizes ablation settings at
$\delta = 0.10$. In this corpus, compatibility-summary features
yield a modest gain over software-only profiles, while CVE and
platform augmentations do not change nearest-neighbor outcomes.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\caption{RQ3 feature ablation at $\delta=0.10$ (n = \enterpriseactiveintrusionsetcount{} intrusion sets).}\label{tab:ablation_rq3}
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{lrr}
\toprule
\textbf{Profile Setting} & \textbf{Unique (\%)} & \textbf{Confused (\%)} \\
\midrule
Software only & \sutprofileuniquesoftwarepercentage\% & \sutprofileconfusionsoftwarepercentage\% \\
Software + CVE & \sutprofileuniquesoftwarecvepercentage\% & \sutprofileconfusionsoftwarecvepercentage\% \\
Software + platform & \sutprofileuniquesoftwareplatformpercentage\% & \sutprofileconfusionsoftwareplatformpercentage\% \\
Software + CVE + platform & \sutprofileuniquesoftwarecveplatformpercentage\% & \sutprofileconfusionsoftwarecveplatformpercentage\% \\
Software + OS family & \sutprofileuniquesoftwarefamilyonlypercentage\% & \sutprofileconfusionsoftwarefamilyonlypercentage\% \\
Software + compatibility summary & \sutprofileuniquesoftwarecompatpercentage\% & \sutprofileconfusionsoftwarecompatpercentage\% \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{figure}[!htb]
\centering
\resizebox{\columnwidth}{!}{\input{figs/ablation_template.tex}}
\caption{RQ3 ablation: confusion rate by profile-setting variant at $\delta=0.10$.}\label{fig:ablation_rq3}
\Description{Horizontal bar chart of confused-group percentages for six profile settings: software only, software plus CVE, software plus platform, software plus CVE plus platform, software plus OS family, and software plus compatibility summary.}
\end{figure}
Figure~\ref{fig:jaccard_cdf} visualizes the full confusion
distribution. The key reading is that adding CVE evidence does not
materially shift nearest-neighbor separability at $\delta = 0.10$.

\begin{figure}[!htb]
\centering
\resizebox{\columnwidth}{!}{\input{figs/jaccard_cdf_template.tex}}
% FIGURE NOTE:
% - Plot both curves using the same intrusion-set population.
% - Mark delta=0.1 with a vertical line and label.
\caption{Nearest-neighbor profile confusion under software-only and software+CVE constraints.}\label{fig:jaccard_cdf}
\Description{CDF of nearest-neighbor Jaccard distance with measured software-only and software-plus-CVE curves. A vertical line marks delta equals 0.1.}
\end{figure}
% LTeX: enabled=true

% ===============================================================
\section{Discussion}\label{sec:discussion}

\subsection{Implications for Reproducible APT Emulation}

Our results establish that structured CTI provides only partial
information for SUT construction. Platform constraints are broadly
populated but coarse; software constraints identify artifacts but
rarely specify versions; and CVE constraints are sparse and split
between structured and free-text fields. Automated SUT generation from CTI alone is therefore
not feasible. The minimum viable workflow requires at least one
analyst-driven enrichment step that pins software versions and maps
platform hints to concrete OS images. This enrichment step is
separate from the procedural gap identified in the companion
study~\cite{ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured}: while
that work shows CTI lacks \emph{how-to-execute} semantics, our
work shows it also lacks \emph{where-to-execute} specifications.

\subsection{Container vs.\ VM Trade-offs}

The compatibility classification reveals that a non-trivial
fraction of Enterprise techniques require VM or full infrastructure
backends beyond what containers provide. Research groups designing
cyber ranges exclusively around container orchestration will
systematically exclude entire tactic clusters, in particular,
boot-level persistence, kernel injection, and enterprise identity
attacks. VM-based environments cover a substantially larger
fraction of the technique space at the cost of greater
infrastructure complexity.

\subsection{SUT Susceptibility and Attribution}

The SUT profile confusion analysis shows that environment-level
profiles are not specific enough to uniquely identify a single
threat actor. A defender who builds a SUT matching the CVE and
software profile of a particular APT group is also building a SUT
susceptible to many other groups. Claims of ``APT-X-specific
emulation'' should therefore be qualified by reporting the SUT
confusion rate at a stated distance threshold.
For attribution-oriented interpretations, a minimum-evidence
threshold should be made explicit before actor-level conclusions.
This paper does not set a universal threshold; it shows that sparse
SUT profiles increase overlap and that threshold selection must be
calibrated using measured confusion behavior. Under the
software-only profile setting, confusion is
\thresholdkoneconfusionpct\% at $k \geq 1$, drops to
\thresholdkthreeconfusionpct\% for profiles with at least three
software links ($n=\thresholdkthreesample$), and reaches
\thresholdkfiveconfusionpct\% for profiles with at least five links
($n=\thresholdkfivesample$).
The delta sensitivity check follows the same direction:
\deltazerozerofiveconfusionpct\% at $\delta=0.05$,
\deltazerotenconfusionpct\% at $\delta=0.10$, and
\deltazerofifteenconfusionpct\% at $\delta=0.15$.

\subsection{Path Toward Machine-Actionable SUT Specifications}

Our measurements identify three concrete gaps and a corresponding
remediation for each.

\myparagraph{Gap 1: software presence without version}
The vast majority of software objects linked to campaigns carry
no version specifier and no CPE identifier. An analyst receiving
a STIX bundle with a \texttt{malware} object named
\emph{Cobalt Strike} cannot derive which server version to
deploy, which listener profile to configure, or which CVEs are
in scope.\ \emph{Remediation:} extend \stix{} \texttt{malware}
and \texttt{tool} objects with optional
\texttt{x\_mitre\_version\_range} and \texttt{cpe23uri} fields,
mirroring the pattern already used by the NVD\@. Adding these two
fields to existing objects requires no schema break and would
directly enable version-pinned SUT construction for the fraction
of software already encoded.

\myparagraph{Gap 2: CVE evidence fragmented across fields}
In the Enterprise corpus, \cvefreetextonlycount{} of
\cveuniquecount{} CVEs (\cvefromfreetextpct\%) appear only in
natural-language \texttt{description} fields, and there are zero
\texttt{vulnerability} SDOs. The remaining evidence is distributed
across structured \texttt{external\_references} entries.
This means automated extraction
requires NLP pipelines whose error rate compounds across a
multi-step SUT derivation workflow; remediation uses LLM-based
enrichment pipelines, operating on the existing corpus of
threat reports that authors cite but do not structure, could
extract CVE identifiers and attach them as structured
\texttt{external\_reference} entries to existing \stix{} objects.
This is a contained, verifiable task suitable for human-in-the-loop
validation at scale.

\myparagraph{Gap 3: no SUT compatibility signal}
The \attack{} schema carries no field indicating whether a
technique requires a container, a VM, or a full enterprise
topology. Our CF/VMR/ID classification is derived heuristically
from platform strings and permission fields, and inherits their
incompleteness.\ \emph{Remediation:} a standardized
\texttt{x\_mitre\_sut\_class} field, populated by the ATT\&CK
team using the same curation process as
\texttt{x\_mitre\_permissions\_required}, would make SUT
feasibility machine-readable and enable cyber range designers to
filter technique sets by deployment cost before planning an
emulation campaign.

\subsection{Threats to Validity}

Construct validity is limited because our measurements reflect
only what is explicitly encoded in STIX fields; version and
configuration information present in referenced threat reports but
not in structured fields is not captured. Internal validity is
constrained by the asynchronous update pipeline between the
ATT\&CK website and downloadable STIX bundles, which may cause
our extracted counts to underestimate true association rates;
the same asynchrony was observed in the companion
study~\cite{ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured}. External
validity is bounded by the focus on public ATT\&CK datasets;
commercial CTI feeds may carry richer software version data.
Campaign-level platform inference also uses software-linked platform
tags as a compatibility proxy. Multi-platform tools can inflate
OS-family counts and do not prove the exact operating system targeted
in a specific incident.
Finally, because this study and its companion analysis share the same
data baseline and extraction conventions, any upstream corpus bias
propagates to both axes; this strengthens comparability but also
couples sensitivity to ATT\&CK curation artifacts.

% ===============================================================
\section{Conclusion}\label{sec:conclusion}

We presented, to the best of our knowledge, the first measurement of \mitre{} \attack{} structured
CTI from the perspective of the \emph{System Under Test}: the
environment that must exist before any emulation procedure can run.
Across the corpora measured for coverage (Enterprise, Mobile, ICS,
CAPEC, and FiGHT), and the Enterprise-focused deployability analyses,
platform constraints are broadly present but coarse; software
references rarely carry version specificity; and CVE linkages are
sparse and split across structured and free-text fields. These findings answer our three
research questions directly: SUT-relevant coverage is incomplete
(RQ1); a significant fraction of techniques require VM or full
infrastructure backends beyond what containers can provide (RQ2);
and SUT profiles built from software and CVE constraints are not
specific enough to distinguish individual threat actors (RQ3).

The companion study showed that structured CTI does not encode
\emph{how} to execute an adversary procedure. This paper shows it
also does not encode \emph{where}. Together, they bound the two
axes of the automation gap between current CTI standards and
reproducible adversary emulation, and identify the concrete
schema extensions needed to close it.
